food_name: Fried Tofu with Gado-Gado Peanut Sauce (6 pieces)

indonesian: Tahu Goreng dengan Saus Kacang Gado-Gado

food_subgroup: Deep-fried soy-based protein with peanut sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried tofu class; no specific entry for Indonesian tahu goreng with gado-gado peanut sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried tofu as a category; no specific code for tahu goreng with gado-gado sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for fried tofu with peanut sauce as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 platter of 6 pieces with gado-gado peanut sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is a platter of 6 pieces of deep-fried tofu (approximately 240 g total including peanut sauce), as typically served at Indonesian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tahu Goreng (Fried Tofu) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried tofu (NDB 16301) and peanut sauce component estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry covers tahu goreng with gado-gado peanut sauce as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) FAO/INFOODS SEA fried tofu values for the tofu component (~65% of dish weight), and (2) USDA SR Legacy peanut sauce / peanut butter-based sauce values for the gado-gado component (~35% of dish weight). Energy and macros are weighted composites. Sodium reflects typical Indonesian peanut sauce seasoning. Values represent a plausible central estimate; restaurant-specific variation is expected.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tofu and peanut-based foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tahu goreng with gado-gado peanut sauce as a composite dish. GI estimated at 50 based on: (1) plain fried tofu GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database; soy-based foods are low GI), and (2) peanut sauce GI ~14 (peanuts GI ~14 per Atkinson et al. 2008). The carbohydrate contribution from palm sugar and tamarind in the sauce raises the composite GI. A blended estimate of GI 50 is applied, reflecting the moderate glycaemic impact of the sugar-containing peanut sauce diluting the very low GI of tofu. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 22.1) / 100 = 11.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning in gado-gado peanut sauce
hypertension.guidance: Each serving of tahu goreng with gado-gado sauce provides approximately 768 mg sodium, which represents about one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-sauce servings is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load driven by palm sugar and tamarind in peanut sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycaemic index of approximately 50 and a glycaemic load of 11 per serving, tahu goreng with gado-gado sauce is acceptable in moderate portions for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the added sugars in the peanut sauce should be factored into total carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat from deep-frying and peanut sauce; favourable unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and soy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tahu goreng provides predominantly unsaturated fats from peanuts and soy, which are cardioprotective, but the deep-frying process adds saturated fat and total energy; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and prefer preparations with less frying oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Tofu with Gado-Gado Peanut Sauce (Tahu Goreng Gado-Gado) is an Indonesian dish consisting of six pieces of deep-fried firm tofu served with a homemade gado-gado peanut sauce. The tofu is made from coagulated soy milk pressed into solid white blocks, then deep-fried in vegetable or palm oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crisp while the interior remains soft and custardy. The gado-gado peanut sauce is a hallmark of Indonesian cuisine, typically prepared from ground roasted peanuts blended with garlic, chilli, tamarind, palm sugar, soy sauce, and water, producing a rich, savoury-sweet, slightly spicy sauce with a thick, glossy consistency. The dish is a popular street food and restaurant item across Indonesia and is widely available in Indonesian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, tahu goreng is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~9.8 g/100 g) from soy, moderate fat (~13.5 g/100 g) predominantly unsaturated from peanuts and soy, and moderate carbohydrate (~9.2 g/100 g) largely from the peanut sauce. Tofu is a good source of calcium and iron. The dish is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is naturally dairy-free. Clinically, the sodium content from the peanut sauce warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, and the added sugars in the sauce should be considered by those managing Type 2 Diabetes. The favourable unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and soy is beneficial for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderation.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry covers tahu goreng with gado-gado peanut sauce as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining FAO/INFOODS SEA fried tofu data with USDA SR Legacy peanut sauce component data. GI is an analogous estimate. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach and expected restaurant-level variation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA (fried tofu component) and USDA SR Legacy (peanut sauce component); no single approved source covers this exact dish. 2. Serving size of 240 g is estimated for 6 pieces of fried tofu plus peanut sauce as typically served; actual restaurant portions may vary. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 for the tofu component; tofu is naturally cholesterol-free (naturally zero). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products (naturally zero). 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this food category. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for fried tofu. 5. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for fried tofu (~15) and peanut-based foods (~14), adjusted upward to reflect the palm sugar and tamarind content of the gado-gado sauce. 6. Allergen note: contains soy and peanuts; soy sauce may introduce gluten. 7. The restaurant serving this dish (Smile Martabak - Funan) is tagged under Indonesian, Stuffed Pancake, Pancake, and Coffee cuisine tags on field survey data; the cuisine tags reflect the restaurant's broader menu rather than this specific dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Expanded food_name from menu-style 'Fried Tofu Platter (6pc)' to canonical 'Fried Tofu with Gado-Gado Peanut Sauce (6 pieces)'.
Added Indonesian local name (Tahu Goreng dengan Saus Kacang Gado-Gado) to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Indonesian cuisine.
Constructed composite nutrient values from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy as no single approved source covers this exact dish.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented zero values (cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg) with naturally zero justification phrases in notes.
Set GI to 50 (numeric > 0) as analogous estimate with full documentation; gi_category low; GL calculated as 11.1.
Used only closest_parent ontology match types; no codes invented; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent — all indonesian and romanized entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
